meta.title: SF-SPSE-3: RBAC, Network Controls, and Compliance Operations

questions.stem: Which TWO design choices improve least-privilege posture? (Select TWO)

options.text: Use job-function roles instead of one shared super-role
Limit sensitive grant capabilities to a small admin set
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all developers for emergencies
Use PUBLIC to own governed objects

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because job-function roles narrow privileges. **B** is correct because sensitive capabilities should be tightly controlled. **C** and **D** weaken governance. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which TWO elements are core parts of a layered Snowflake security architecture? (Select TWO)

options.text: Data protection policies such as masking or row access

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because identity and RBAC are foundational. **B** is correct because data protection policies add defense in depth. **C** removes visibility, and **D** is not a security architecture principle. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A security engineer wants Snowflake-hosted code to call an external API safely. Which feature area should they study in addition to classic network policies?

options.text: External network access integrations and rules

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because outbound calls from supported code rely on external network access features, not just inbound login policies. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not solve outbound connectivity governance. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/external-network-access/external-network-access-overview

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about OAuth in Snowflake are generally correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: OAuth can support token-based access patterns for applications
OAuth replaces all Snowflake roles and privileges
OAuth can be relevant for service-to-service scenarios

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because OAuth enables token-based application access. **C** is correct because service-to-service patterns can use OAuth flows. **B** is false because RBAC still applies, and **D** is false because network controls remain useful. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/oauth-intro

questions.stem: Which authentication method lets a service account connect to Snowflake without storing a password, using an RSA key pair instead?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because Snowflake supports RSA key-pair authentication for programmatic access. **A** is federated SSO, **C** is not a primary auth method, and **D** is a network restriction, not authentication. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth

questions.stem: What is a key difference between network policies and external network access rules?

options.text: Network policies govern inbound login IP restrictions, while external network access rules govern outbound connections from supported code
They are identical features with different names
External network access rules manage warehouse sizing
Network policies are only for Tableau integrations

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because network policies protect inbound authentication and external network access rules control outbound connectivity from supported workloads. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/external-network-access/external-network-access-overview

questions.stem: Which privilege model concept explains why object creators can accidentally create grant sprawl in regular schemas?

options.text: Object owners can grant privileges on what they own

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because object ownership includes grant authority in regular schemas. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which view is best for tracing which objects a query read or modified during a security investigation?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `ACCESS_HISTORY` provides object-level access auditing for queries. **A** is less detailed for object lineage, **C** is role metadata, and **D** is configuration, not evidence of access. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history

questions.stem: A company must show only each tenant’s own rows from a shared table. Which feature is the best fit?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because row access policies filter visible rows based on context. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not implement row-level data isolation. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature masks sensitive column values at query time based on policy logic?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because dynamic data masking applies policy-based masking to column results. **A** filters rows, **C** manages credits, and **D** controls session behavior. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake role hierarchies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Privileges flow from child roles to parent roles that contain them
Granting every user ACCOUNTADMIN is a best practice for troubleshooting
Custom roles should usually be designed around responsibilities
OWNERSHIP can never be inherited through a granted role

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because parent roles inherit from granted child roles. **C** is correct because responsibility-based role design supports least privilege. **B** is unsafe, and **D** is false because inherited roles can convey privileges including ownership-derived capabilities. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the main difference in outcome between a row access policy and a masking policy when a query runs?

options.text: Row access policies hide rows; masking policies alter visible values
Masking policies hide rows; row access policies resize warehouses
They always produce the same result

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because row access governs row visibility while masking changes returned column values. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-row-intro

questions.stem: A masking policy reveals full values only to approved roles, while all other roles see partially obscured output. What security principle does this illustrate?

options.text: Least privilege and role-based data exposure

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because masking uses role-aware policy logic to expose only what is necessary. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro

questions.stem: An enterprise wants customer-managed key participation, federated identity, private connectivity, and data masking. What does this combination represent?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because combining identity, network, encryption, and data protection controls creates layered security. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption-tss

questions.stem: Which design best supports separation of duties in Snowflake?

options.text: One shared admin role for everyone
Giving analysts ownership of security policies

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because separation of duties means splitting object administration and security administration where appropriate. **A**, **C**, and **D** violate that principle. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which kind of Snowflake evidence is most helpful for showing whether MFA was involved in a login event?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because login history contains authentication-related connection details. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history

questions.stem: Which system role is typically used to manage grants, roles, and users in Snowflake?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because SECURITYADMIN is the standard role for security administration in an account. **A** focuses on object management, **C** is not an admin role, and **D** is organization-scoped. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What does a network policy restrict in Snowflake?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because network policies restrict authentication by client IP. **A**, **C**, and **D** are different controls. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: What is the main security value of automatic sensitive data classification and tags in Snowflake?

options.text: They eliminate the need for users
They help discover and govern sensitive data consistently at scale

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because classification and tags support scalable discovery, labeling, and downstream policy application. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/classify-auto

questions.stem: A security team needs to prove whether a masked column was accessed by a user yesterday. What is the most relevant evidence source first?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because access history provides the audit trail for object reads, which can be combined with masking policy context. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not show data access events. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history

questions.stem: Why is `MANAGE GRANTS` considered sensitive from a security perspective?

options.text: It enables centralized privilege administration across objects
It encrypts data with customer keys

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `MANAGE GRANTS` allows broad grant administration, so it should be limited carefully. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which view should you query first to review failed logins and MFA-related connection details?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because `LOGIN_HISTORY` records authentication attempts and connection metadata. **A** is for data access, and **C** and **D** are usage/storage views. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history

questions.stem: Which feature is best for protecting sensitive values like national IDs while still allowing broad table access patterns?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because dynamic data masking protects column values without necessarily removing row access. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not protect data values directly. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro

questions.stem: A contractor needs tighter login restrictions than employees, without changing the account-wide policy for everyone else. What is the cleanest approach?

options.text: Assign a user-level network policy to the contractor
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN so the contractor can self-manage access

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because user-level network policies support targeted restrictions for a specific identity. **B** is unsafe, **C** weakens security, and **D** is unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Why is broad use of ACCOUNTADMIN discouraged even in mature environments?

options.text: It concentrates too much privilege and complicates auditability

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because concentrated privilege increases blast radius and weakens separation of duties. **A**, **C**, and **D** are false. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Which authentication setup is commonly used for browser-based workforce SSO into Snowflake?

options.text: SAML 2.0 with the enterprise identity provider
OAuth client credentials for every human user in the browser

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because SAML federation is the common browser SSO approach. **B** is encryption-related, **C** is compute-related, and **D** is not the standard human SSO pattern. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of federated SSO for enterprise security teams?

options.text: It removes the need for RBAC
It centralizes authentication with the identity provider

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because SSO centralizes authentication and lifecycle control in the IdP. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect because RBAC and network controls still matter. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview

questions.stem: A global enterprise must align Snowflake access with HR identity lifecycle events such as joiner, mover, and leaver processes. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Manual local passwords for each user
Federated identity integration with role assignment governance
Only network policies without identity provider integration

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because federated identity integration centralizes lifecycle control and works well with governed role assignment. **A**, **C**, and **D** are weak security designs. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview

questions.stem: A security engineer wants a non-person user that can call Snowflake from automation with no interactive password login. What is the most appropriate setup?

options.text: Create a human user with ACCOUNTADMIN
Use key-pair authentication for the service account
Use a network policy without any credential mechanism

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because service access should use a dedicated principal with a supported non-password authentication mechanism such as key pairs. **A** violates least privilege, **C** is not authentication, and **D** still lacks identity proof. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/key-pair-auth

questions.stem: Which TWO practices improve Snowflake compliance posture? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply consistent tags and governance policies to sensitive data
Ignore login telemetry if SSO is enabled

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because regular audit review supports governance. **C** is correct because consistent tagging and policy application improve control coverage. **B** and **D** weaken compliance. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/access_history

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly distinguish masking policies from row access policies? (Select TWO)

options.text: Row access policies decide which rows are visible
Masking policies can suspend a warehouse
Row access policies replace RBAC completely

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because masking policies alter returned column values. **B** is correct because row access policies govern row visibility. **C** and **D** confuse data protection with compute control or core authorization. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-ddm-intro

questions.stem: Which control should be used to limit allowed source IP ranges for all users by default?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because an account-level network policy establishes the default IP-based login boundary. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: Which role is usually preferred for routine object creation and management rather than account-wide security administration?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because SYSADMIN is intended for object administration. **B** focuses on security administration, **C** is organization-scoped, and **D** is not an admin role. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: Why might a company adopt PrivateLink or Private Endpoint for Snowflake connectivity?

options.text: To keep traffic off the public internet
To replace MFA with private DNS
To create new custom roles automatically
To speed up Time Travel recovery

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because private connectivity reduces exposure to the public internet. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not the purpose of the feature. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/privatelink-overview

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake network policies are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: They can be assigned at account or user level
They can enforce IP allow/block logic
They grant access to schemas when the IP matches

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because policies can be assigned broadly or per user. **B** is correct because they implement IP filtering. **C** and **D** confuse network controls with data protection and RBAC. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies

questions.stem: A company wants interactive users to authenticate through their enterprise identity provider. Which Snowflake capability is the standard fit?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because SAML 2.0 federation is the standard Snowflake SSO pattern for enterprise IdPs. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-security-fed-auth-overview

questions.stem: Which feature adds customer-managed key participation to Snowflake encryption while Snowflake still maintains its own keys?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Tri-Secret Secure incorporates a customer-managed key with Snowflake’s encryption model. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-encryption-tss

questions.stem: A security engineer wants to correlate a suspicious login with later object access. Which two history areas are most useful together?
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because login telemetry and object access telemetry together support incident reconstruction. The other pairs do not provide the same audit trail. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/account-usage/login_history

questions.stem: Which TWO controls are configured through Snowflake password or login policies? (Select TWO)
questions.explanation: **A** is correct because password policies can enforce complexity rules. **B** is correct because login policies can control failed login behavior. **C** and **D** are unrelated account settings. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/admin-user-management

questions.stem: Which Snowflake principle is most directly implemented by building custom roles for job functions and granting only needed privileges?
questions.explanation: **B** is correct because role design around job functions implements least privilege. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated platform features. https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview
